ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for seafood pasta baked in parchment; 'Pasta, cooked' (A00LC) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for marinara al cartoccio or sufficiently specific seafood pasta dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Marinara al Cartoccio is approximately 380 g, comprising a generous portion of spaghetti with mixed seafood (prawns, mussels, calamari, clams) in cherry tomato sauce, sealed and baked in parchment paper.

nutrient_source.title: Pasta with seafood, cooked — composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked spaghetti (FDC ID 168936), mixed cooked seafood components (shrimp FDC ID 175180, mussels FDC ID 175168, squid FDC ID 175185, clams FDC ID 175167), and cherry tomato sauce analogue (FDC ID 170457), weighted by typical recipe proportions.
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Marinara al Cartoccio. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for the primary components: cooked spaghetti (~45% by weight), mixed seafood (prawns, mussels, calamari, clams, ~35% by weight), cherry tomato sauce (~15% by weight), and olive oil/garlic/parsley (~5% by weight). Proportions are based on standard Italian restaurant recipe ratios. Values represent per-100 g of the finished dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — spaghetti, cooked al dente (GI ~45, reference glucose); seafood components have negligible GI contribution.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 45 is assigned based on the University of Sydney GI Database value for cooked spaghetti al dente (GI ~45, glucose reference = 100), which constitutes the dominant glycemic carbohydrate source in this dish. Seafood, tomato sauce, and olive oil contribute negligible glycemic load. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 55.1) / 100 ≈ 24.8, where carbohydrate per 380 g serving = 14.5 × 3.8 = 55.1 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from seafood and tomato sauce
hypertension.guidance: A 380 g restaurant serving provides approximately 1178 mg sodium, representing nearly half the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size to manage sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from spaghetti carbohydrates
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic index of 45 (low) but a glycemic load of approximately 24.8 per restaurant serving, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size carefully, as the total carbohydrate load from spaghetti can meaningfully raise postprandial blood glucose despite the relatively low GI of al dente pasta.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, high omega-3 from seafood
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The mixed seafood components (mussels, prawns, calamari, clams) provide beneficial omega-3 fatty acids and lean protein with low saturated fat (~0.5 g per 100 g), supporting a heart-healthy dietary pattern when prepared with minimal added oil and moderate sodium.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Marinara al Cartoccio is a classic Italian seafood pasta dish in which spaghetti is combined with a medley of fresh seafood — typically prawns, mussels, calamari (squid), and clams — in a cherry tomato sauce flavoured with garlic, white wine, olive oil, and Italian parsley, then sealed inside parchment paper or aluminium foil and baked in an oven. The 'al cartoccio' technique (Italian for 'in a parcel') traps steam inside the sealed parcel, gently finishing the seafood and infusing the pasta with the concentrated aromas of the sea and tomato. The result is a dish with tender, slightly firm spaghetti, succulent mixed shellfish, and a light, fragrant tomato broth. Texturally, the pasta retains a slight bite (al dente) while the seafood remains moist and delicate. Marinara al Cartoccio is a staple of Italian coastal cuisine and is widely served in Italian restaurants internationally, including in Singapore where it appears on menus such as Ricciotti. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-calorie, high-protein meal (~118 kcal and ~9.2 g protein per 100 g) with low saturated fat and a meaningful contribution of vitamin B12, iron, selenium, and omega-3 fatty acids from the seafood. The glycemic index is low (~45) due to al dente spaghetti, though the glycemic load per restaurant serving is moderate (~24.8), requiring portion awareness for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium content is moderate to high in restaurant preparations, warranting caution for those with hypertension.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Marinara al Cartoccio as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted USDA FoodData Central composite of the primary ingredient components (cooked spaghetti, mixed seafood, cherry tomato sauce, olive oil) using standard Italian restaurant recipe proportions. GI is assigned by analogy to cooked spaghetti al dente from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods entries for individual components; no single approved-source record for this exact dish exists. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component entries used. 3. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g per 100 g; this is confirmed zero by formulation as the dish contains no hydrogenated fats — olive oil and seafood are the primary fat sources. 4. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on cooked spaghetti al dente from the University of Sydney GI Database; the seafood and tomato sauce components contribute negligible glycemic carbohydrates. 5. Serving size of 380 g is estimated from typical Italian restaurant portion norms; actual restaurant portion at Ricciotti may vary. 6. Halal status is marked false as shellfish (mussels, clams) are not considered halal under most Islamic jurisprudence schools, and restaurant halal certification is not confirmed. 7. The cuisine_tag 'Chicken Dish' in the input record is incorrect for this dish and has been disregarded. 8. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA component data and carry medium confidence. 9. This is an Italian cuisine dish served in Singapore; multilingual naming rules for Singaporean dishes do not apply as this is not a Singaporean dish by origin — Italian cuisine does not require Chinese, Malay, Tamil, or Hindi local names under the Veritas multilingual matrix.
